At the clinic, treatment is mainly supportive and may include decontamination if recent and safe, fluids, warmth, and monitoring of heart rate and temperature until the effects wear off, with additional care if other toxins were involved; discuss exact treatment and dosing with your veterinarian. Most pets recover fully with supportive care. Prevent exposure by storing all cannabis products securely out of reach, just as you would any medication.

title: Allergic reactions and anaphylaxis in dogs and cats
content: Pets can have allergic reactions ranging from mild and annoying to sudden and life-threatening, and recognizing the difference helps you respond appropriately. Reactions are often triggered by insect stings or bites, certain medications or vaccines, foods, or other allergens. Mild to moderate reactions commonly cause facial swelling, especially around the muzzle and eyes, raised itchy bumps called hives across the skin, redness, intense itching, and sometimes vomiting or diarrhea. While alarming to see, these milder reactions are usually not immediately life-threatening, but they still warrant prompt veterinary attention because they can worsen. The most severe form, anaphylaxis, is a rapid, whole-body reaction that is a true emergency. In pets it can cause sudden weakness or collapse, difficulty breathing, pale gums, vomiting and diarrhea, a drop in blood pressure leading to shock, and, in dogs especially, signs may center on the digestive tract and circulation. Anaphylaxis can develop within minutes of exposure and can be fatal without rapid treatment. If your pet suddenly collapses, struggles to breathe, has pale gums, or rapidly worsens after a sting, new medication, or other exposure, treat it as an emergency and get to a veterinarian or emergency clinic immediately, calling ahead. For milder reactions such as facial swelling or hives in a pet that is breathing comfortably and acting fairly normally, contact your veterinarian promptly for guidance, and watch closely for any progression to breathing trouble or collapse, which means you should go in at once. Do not give human allergy medications on your own, because dosing and product choice matter and some products are not safe for pets; let your veterinarian direct any treatment. If a stinger is visible, you can gently scrape it away. At the clinic, treatment depends on severity and may include medications to calm the reaction, support for breathing and blood pressure, fluids, and monitoring; discuss exact treatment and dosing with your veterinarian. If your pet has had a serious reaction before, ask your veterinarian about an action plan for future exposures.

title: Skin wounds and abscesses in dogs and cats
content: Skin wounds and abscesses are common in pets, particularly in cats that roam or get into fights, and recognizing them early helps prevent painful complications. Wounds can result from bites, scratches, cuts, scrapes, and punctures. Bite wounds are especially important because the small puncture in the skin can seal over while bacteria are trapped underneath, leading to an abscess, which is a painful pocket of infection and pus that develops over days. Cats commonly develop abscesses after fights, often on the face, legs, or base of the tail. Signs of a wound or developing abscess include a visible cut or puncture, swelling, redness, heat, and pain in an area, a soft fluid-filled lump, discharge or a foul odor, matted fur over a sore spot, licking or grooming at one area, and more general signs such as fever, lethargy, and reduced appetite as the infection takes hold. Some abscesses rupture and drain on their own, leaving an open, oozing sore. Most wounds and abscesses are not immediate emergencies, but they are uncomfortable and tend to worsen without care, so prompt veterinary attention is wise, especially for puncture and bite wounds, anything swollen, hot, or draining, large or deep wounds, wounds near the eyes or over the chest or abdomen, and any wound accompanied by fever or lethargy. Heavy bleeding or a large gaping wound should be treated urgently. For minor surface wounds, you can gently clean the area with mild saline if your pet tolerates it and prevent licking, but avoid using strong antiseptics, alcohol, or human ointments without veterinary advice, and never give human pain medications, which can be toxic. Do not try to drain an abscess yourself. At the clinic, the veterinarian will clean and assess the wound, may need to drain an abscess and address infection, and will recommend appropriate care to help it heal; discuss exact treatment and dosing with your veterinarian. Keeping outdoor cats indoors or supervised and staying current on vaccines helps reduce fight wounds and their complications.

title: Dental disease and oral pain in dogs and cats
content: Dental disease is one of the most widespread health problems in dogs and cats, and because pets often hide oral pain, it is frequently overlooked until it is advanced. It begins when plaque and tartar build up on the teeth and along the gumline, leading to inflammation of the gums and, over time, infection and damage to the structures that support the teeth. This can cause chronic pain, loose or abscessed teeth, and bacteria entering the bloodstream, which may affect overall health. Cats can also develop specific painful dental conditions, and both species commonly suffer in silence while continuing to eat. Signs to watch for include bad breath, which is often the earliest clue, yellow or brown tartar on the teeth, red, swollen, or bleeding gums, drooling, pawing at the mouth, dropping food or chewing on one side, reluctance to eat hard food, facial swelling, and a decline in grooming in cats. Some pets become withdrawn or irritable because of ongoing discomfort. Dental disease is rarely an emergency, but a facial swelling under the eye, signs of significant mouth pain, or a pet that suddenly stops eating warrants prompt veterinary attention, and any cat that stops eating needs timely care. Because much dental disease lies below the gumline, it cannot be fully assessed or treated at home; a thorough evaluation and professional cleaning under anesthesia, often with dental X-rays, is needed to find and address problems. Do not attempt to scrape tartar yourself or give human pain medications, which can be toxic to pets. At the clinic, the veterinarian will examine the mouth, recommend professional dental care as appropriate, and treat painful or diseased teeth; discuss exact treatment and dosing with your veterinarian. Between professional cleanings, you can support oral health at home with veterinary-recommended measures such as tooth brushing with pet-safe products and approved dental treats. Regular checkups allow problems to be caught early, before they cause significant pain or tooth loss, keeping your pet comfortable and healthy.
